FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study participants at 21 U.S. sites, ages 11-12 years at Year 2 (2018-2020)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using linear and logistic regression models to examine associations between exposures at Year 2 and outcomes at Year 3

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in American Journal of Preventive Medicine, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper measures problematic smartphone/SM-specific variables (MPIQ [Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire], SMAQ [Social Media Addiction Questionnaire], VGAQ [Video Game Addiction Questionnaire]) as exposures paired with individual-trait-level outcomes. MPIQ and SMAQ measure smartphone/SM use patterns and are paired with mental health outcomes (CBCL depressive, somatic, attention/deficit, oppositional defiant, conduct problems scales), suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, and substance use initiation. These satisfy Step 1 requirement.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression analyses report quantitative results: β coefficients and odds ratios for associations between problematic screen use scores and mental health outcomes; e.g., MPIQ/SMAQ associated with higher depressive problems, suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, substance initiation

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear and logistic regression models report numeric estimates (β, OR) for SM/phone variables with individual-trait outcomes across main results tables

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (Year 2 exposure → Year 3 outcome), but SM/phone problematic use is the IV (predictor), not DV-only. EC7 requires SM as DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere. Here SM is the IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper examines prospective associations between problematic screen use (MPIQ, SMAQ, VGAQ) and mental/behavioral health outcomes in early adolescents. All three problematic-use measures are SM/phone variables paired with individual-trait outcomes meeting IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data from baseline (2016-2018) to Year 2 (2018-2020) follow-up. N=7528 adolescents at 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis using group-based trajectory modeling to estimate patterns of daily social media use from baseline to Year 2, with linear regression models examining associations with cognitive outcomes at Year 2

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Lancet Regional Health - Americas, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper measures daily social media time (hours/day spent on Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, etc.) assessed at baseline, Year 1, and Year 2. Trajectory modeling creates SM-specific exposure groups paired with individual-trait-level cognitive outcomes (Little Man Task accuracy, Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test measures including initial learning, retroactive interference, long delay recall). Table 2 shows B coefficients for SM trajectory groups predicting cognitive performance. Phone/SM-specific variable with individual-trait outcome. MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models report B coefficients with 95% CIs for social media trajectories predicting cognitive outcomes; e.g., low increasing SM use: B=-1.38 (95% CI: -1.82, -0.94) for RAVLT Initial Trials; high increasing: B=-1.90 (95% CI: -2.76, -1.04)

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported for all cognitive outcome measures: regression B coefficients with confidence intervals and p-values for Little Man Task and RAVLT subtests

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline to Year 2), but social media use is the IV (trajectory exposure variable), not DV. Cognitive performance is the DV. EC7 requires SM as DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV anywhere. Here SM is the IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper uses group-based trajectory modeling to identify three social media use patterns (no/very low, low increasing, high increasing) as predictors of cognitive performance. Social media is measured as hours/day on named platforms (Instagram, Facebook, Twitter) and serves as the independent variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort study analyzes data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at 21 U.S. sites, baseline 2016-2019, ages 9-11 years, using ABCD Study release 5.0

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis performing genetic sensitivity analyses (Gsens) with structural equation models to estimate genetic confounding in associations between child screen time and psychiatric problems

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open, November 6, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search: Primary exposure is "child-reported daily screen time (in hours)" without modality breakdown in main analysis. Step 2 composite check: The screen time measure aggregates TV/video watching and video gaming without isolating phone/SM components. Methods state GWAS used "leisure television watching time" as proxy for screen time; sensitivity analyses examine TV watching and gaming subtypes separately but main analysis pools modalities. Per binding clarification (§2.4): "Multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2" and the composite includes TV and console/PC gaming without separate per-modality estimates in primary analysis. This is generic screen time, not phone/SM-specific. NOT_MET.

notes: Paper examines genetic confounding in associations between child screen time and psychiatric problems (attention and internalizing problems) using Gsens models with polygenic risk scores and heritability estimates. The primary screen time measure is a broad composite of TV/video and gaming without modality-specific breakdown in main analysis, failing FT-IC2.
